_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Pulaski County, MO. The site of Fort Leonard Wood — the single most transformative event in the county's history. Before 1940, Pulaski County was one of the most isolated, poorest counties in Missouri. The Army condemned thousands of acres of Ozark farmland, displaced hundreds of families, and built a military base that permanently altered the county's economy, demographics, and landscape. Route 66 runs through the county. The Gasconade and Big Piney rivers define the terrain.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Salem Plateau (Ozark Highlands). Classic dissected Ozark plateau terrain — deeply incised valleys, spring-fed streams, narrow ridgetops. The Big Piney River, Gasconade River, and Roubidoux Creek define the major drainage patterns.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 700–1,400 ft. Extreme local relief — narrow ridges drop 300-500 ft into river valleys. Some of the most rugged terrain in the cluster.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Deeply dissected Ozark plateau. The Gasconade River flows through the northern part of the county; the Big Piney River flows through the center. Roubidoux Creek flows through Waynesville. The terrain is so rugged that road construction was nearly impossible before the twentieth century — travel followed creek bottoms and ridge roads that were impassable in wet weather.

soil_types.description: Standard Ozark cherty soils on ridges and slopes. Extremely rocky, thin, and acidic.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: The poorest agricultural soils in the cluster. Pulaski County families farmed where they could find flat ground — in the narrow creek bottoms — and ran hogs on the ridges. The land supported subsistence, barely.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Pulaski County, Missouri (1981).

major_waterways.name: Gasconade River
major_waterways.description: The Gasconade River flows through northern Pulaski County in a broad valley. Spring-fed, clear, and significant for timber floating in the nineteenth century.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Gasconade in Pulaski County was the one reliable thing the land offered — clear water, gravel bottom, fish for the taking, and a current that would carry logs to market when nothing else could move through the hills.

major_waterways.name: Big Piney River
major_waterways.role: Central drainage, mill power, Fort Leonard Wood water supply
major_waterways.description: Flows through the center of the county, directly through the area that became Fort Leonard Wood. The Army's appropriation of the Big Piney watershed displaced the farm families who had depended on it for generations.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Big Piney ran through the heart of what became Fort Leonard Wood — {ancestor_name}'s family may have farmed its banks for three generations before the Army came and told them the water, the land, and the hollow no longer belonged to them.

major_waterways.name: Roubidoux Creek
major_waterways.description: Flows through Waynesville, the county seat. Roubidoux Spring, one of the largest springs in the Ozarks, feeds the creek — a massive outpouring of cold, clear water from the karst bedrock.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: Roubidoux Spring poured out of the hillside below Waynesville like the Ozarks bleeding clear water — {ancestor_name} drank from a spring that produced millions of gallons a day from bedrock older than any human memory.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Dense Ozark oak-hickory forest; fire-maintained savannas on ridgetops; canebrakes in river bottoms
vegetation_eras.description: Pulaski County was among the most heavily forested areas in the Ozarks. The rugged terrain discouraged clearing. Osage and later Delaware peoples used the river valleys for seasonal camps.
vegetation_eras.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Small clearings in creek bottoms for corn; ridgetop forest gradually thinned by timber cutting and charcoal production
vegetation_eras.description: Settlement barely dented the forest. Clearings were small and scattered, concentrated in the wider creek bottoms. The timber industry cut selectively but the terrain made hauling timber out difficult. By 1940, Pulaski County was still more forest than farm.
vegetation_eras.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Fort Leonard Wood construction cleared thousands of acres; military training altered landscape; remaining farms declined
vegetation_eras.description: The construction of Fort Leonard Wood (1940-1941) was the most dramatic landscape change in the county's history. Thousands of acres of forest and farmland were cleared for barracks, roads, training ranges, and artillery impact areas. The Army reshaped the terrain itself — grading ridges, filling hollows, building roads where no road had ever gone.
vegetation_eras.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

sensory_snapshots.season: Summer in the Big Piney valley before Fort Leonard Wood
sensory_snapshots.snapshot: In July in the Big Piney valley, the world was green and still. The creek ran clear over gravel, cool enough to wade barefoot and deep enough in the pools to baptize. The air smelled of hot timber and wild mint growing along the banks. A farm family's clearing was a patch of corn in the narrow bottom, fenced with split rails against the hogs that ran loose in the timber. The nearest neighbor was a half-mile upstream, the nearest store was five miles by creek-bottom trail, and the nearest doctor was in Waynesville — a day's ride. This was the Ozarks at its most isolated: beautiful, self-contained, and desperately poor.
sensory_snapshots.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: In January 1941, the Big Piney valley that had been silent for a century exploded with sound — bulldozers grinding through frozen Ozark clay, dynamite blasting ridges flat, trucks on roads that had been creek-bottom trails a month before. The Army was building Fort Leonard Wood at wartime speed, and the families who had lived in the hollows were gone — bought out, condemned out, or simply told to leave. The smell was diesel exhaust and fresh-cut timber and the raw mineral smell of exposed dolomite. Thousands of construction workers — most from outside the Ozarks — slept in temporary barracks while they built the permanent ones. The contrast with what had stood there six months before was absolute: where a family had grown corn beside a clear creek, a barracks now housed a hundred soldiers who had never seen a mule.
sensory_snapshots.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: In November, the hog drive on the ridge road above the Gasconade was the closest thing Pulaski County had to a commercial event. Farmers drove their semi-wild hogs — fattened on acorn mast through the fall — along the ridge trails toward the railroad at Rolla or the river crossing at Waynesville. The hogs were lean, long-legged razorbacks that could travel ten miles a day on their own feet. The smell was hog — unwashed, acorn-fed, angry. The drovers walked behind, keeping the hogs on the trail with dogs and switches. This was the only cash crop the Ozark uplands produced in quantity: hogs that fed themselves on the forest floor.
sensory_snapshots.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

landscape_changes.description: The Delaware (Lenape) people were relocated to southwestern Missouri, including portions of what became Pulaski County, in the 1820s as part of federal removal policies. They were subsequently pushed further west by the 1830s. This was a second displacement — the Delaware had already been removed from their eastern homeland.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The land {ancestor_name} settled in Pulaski County may have been Delaware territory as recently as a decade before — a people displaced twice, first from the East and then from the Ozarks, their presence erased from the landscape but not from the treaties.
landscape_changes.source: Weslager, C. A. The Delaware Indians: A History. New Brunswick, NJ: Rutgers University Press, 1972.

landscape_changes.change: Fort Leonard Wood construction displaces Ozark families
landscape_changes.description: The federal government condemned approximately 65,000 acres in Pulaski County to build Fort Leonard Wood. Hundreds of families — many of whom had lived in the hollows for three or four generations — were displaced. The government paid appraised value for the land, but the prices reflected Ozark subsistence-farm valuations: often less than five dollars an acre. Families who had known no other home were scattered to Waynesville, Rolla, St. Louis, and beyond. Cemeteries were relocated. Churches were demolished. The hollows that had been home became artillery ranges.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s family was displaced by Fort Leonard Wood, the loss was not measured in dollars per acre — it was measured in generations of burial grounds, cabin sites, and creek-bottom farms that became training ranges overnight.
landscape_changes.source: Rafferty, The Ozarks: Land and Life (2001).

narrative_hooks: Fort Leonard Wood swallowed the hollows whole — {ancestor_name}'s family may have farmed the Big Piney valley for four generations before the Army arrived in 1940 and turned the corn patch into a rifle range.

Pulaski County before Fort Leonard Wood was the Ozarks at their most isolated — no paved roads, no electricity, no doctor closer than a day's ride. After the Army came, the isolation ended overnight, along with the way of life it had sustained.
The Big Piney ran through the heart of what the Army wanted — clear water, flat terrain, and people too poor and too few to stop the condemnation.
Route 66 through Pulaski County carried soldiers to Fort Leonard Wood — farm families on the ridges watched convoys pass below and knew their world had changed in ways the hollow could not absorb.
The Delaware had been promised the Ozarks as a new home — and then removed again within a decade, displaced twice by a government that specialized in temporary promises.
source_references: Rafferty, Milton D. The Ozarks: Land and Life. 2nd ed. Fayetteville: University of Arkansas Press, 2001.
Weslager, C. A. The Delaware Indians: A History. New Brunswick, NJ: Rutgers University Press, 1972.
Wallis, Michael. Route 66: The Mother Road. New York: St. Martin's Press, 1990.
Federal Writers' Project. Missouri: The WPA Guide to the Show Me State. New York: Duell, Sloan and Pearce, 1941.
USDA Soil Survey of Pulaski County, Missouri. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1981.
